name: Calmerry
tagline: Budget-conscious therapy with transparent pricing and no hidden fees
verdict_short: Calmerry competes on pricing transparency. What you see is what you pay, with CBT-based tools included. Smaller therapist network than BetterHelp but honest pricing.
body: Calmerry is one of the few online therapy platforms where the pricing is genuinely transparent upfront — no bait-and-switch after signup, no unexplained billing cycles. Plans start at /week, include asynchronous messaging and live sessions, and come with built-in CBT worksheets and tools. The therapist network is smaller than BetterHelp or Talkspace, but the quality filtering is solid. Insurance is not accepted, making this a self-pay platform only. For people paying out of pocket who are frustrated by opaque pricing elsewhere, Calmerry offers a clear-cut alternative.
best_for: People paying out of pocket who want predictable, transparent costs
Clients who value async messaging between live sessions
Those wanting CBT-based tools built into their subscription
look_elsewhere: If you need insurance coverage — Calmerry is self-pay only
If you want the widest possible therapist selection
If you need psychiatry or medication management
pricing_detail: Calmerry's pricing is refreshingly simple: -70/week depending on the plan tier, billed monthly. Higher tiers include more live sessions per month. All plans include unlimited async messaging. There are no hidden fees and no post-signup price changes — what you see during signup is what you'll pay.
formats_detail: Calmerry supports video sessions, live chat sessions, and async text messaging. The combination makes it flexible for different communication preferences — you can do a formal video session one week and lean on messaging support the next.
pull_quote: Pricing transparency isn't glamorous, but after signing up for platforms that bury their real costs, knowing exactly what you'll pay from day one matters.

name: Cerebral
tagline: Medication management platform — psychiatry-first with therapy as an add-on
verdict_short: Cerebral has had a troubled history with regulatory scrutiny around stimulant prescribing. They've restructured but the reputation hit is real. Solid for non-controlled medication management.
body: Cerebral connects patients with psychiatric providers for medication management and offers therapy as an optional add-on. The platform has faced serious regulatory scrutiny — including a DEA investigation related to stimulant prescribing practices during 2021-2022 — and has significantly restructured its prescribing protocols since then. The company's reputation took a real hit, and it's worth knowing about before you sign up. That said, for non-controlled medication management (anxiety, depression, certain mood disorders), Cerebral's restructured model is functional and more affordable than many alternatives. We'd recommend it for that narrower use case with eyes open to the history.
best_for: Adults needing psychiatric medication management for non-controlled conditions
Insured patients looking for a lower-cost psychiatry option
Those who want therapy and medication on the same platform
look_elsewhere: If you have concerns about the platform's regulatory history
If you specifically need ADHD stimulant management — prescribing protocols are stricter now
If you want a clean reputation without controversy
pricing_detail: Cerebral's pricing depends on services selected. Medication management only starts around /month; combined therapy and psychiatry plans run up to /month. Several insurance plans are accepted. Verify your specific carrier before signing up, as in-network status varies.
formats_detail: Cerebral uses video for psychiatric evaluations and therapy sessions, with messaging available between appointments. The video-first model is appropriate for the clinical nature of medication management — async messaging works better as a supplement than a primary mode.
pull_quote: Knowing a platform's history isn't a reason to automatically disqualify it, but it is a reason to go in with clear expectations about what you're choosing and why.

name: Circle Medical
tagline: Primary care telehealth with integrated mental health — one provider relationship
verdict_short: Circle Medical is a primary care telehealth platform with mental health as one service. Good if you want one provider for both physical and mental health. Not a therapy-depth platform.
body: Circle Medical is a primary care telehealth practice — not a dedicated mental health platform. Mental health services are part of what Circle Medical providers offer alongside urgent care, chronic disease management, and general medicine. The appeal is consolidation: if you want to see one provider who can manage your anxiety, your blood pressure, and your annual physical without hopping between platforms, Circle Medical makes that possible. Most major insurance plans are accepted, and the per-visit cost with insurance is comparable to a standard office copay. The limitation is depth — Circle Medical's mental health providers are generalists, not specialists, and ongoing weekly therapy is not the model.
best_for: People who want mental health integrated with general primary care
Insured patients who want a single telehealth provider for multiple needs
Those with mild mental health concerns who don't need a dedicated therapist
look_elsewhere: Anyone wanting deep ongoing therapy work with a dedicated specialist
People with complex psychiatric needs or specialized conditions
Those who want messaging, coaching, or async between-session support
pricing_detail: Circle Medical operates on a standard insurance billing model. With most major plans, you'll pay your normal telehealth copay — typically -50 per visit. Self-pay rates are available but priced closer to traditional medical visits. The platform is best valued as a covered medical service, not a self-pay therapy subscription.
formats_detail: All appointments are video-based, conducted through the Circle Medical app or browser. There is no messaging tier, no async support, and no phone option. Each appointment is a standalone clinical encounter, not an ongoing care program.
pull_quote: The case for Circle Medical is consolidation — fewer platforms, fewer logins, and mental health treated as a component of whole-person care rather than a separate system.

name: Doctor on Demand
tagline: Established telehealth brand with solid insurance coverage for psychiatric evaluation
verdict_short: Doctor on Demand is a legacy telehealth platform with solid insurance coverage. Better for psychiatric evaluation and urgent needs than ongoing weekly therapy.
body: Doctor on Demand is one of the original telehealth platforms — established before the pandemic-era surge — and its longevity shows in its insurance integrations. Most major carriers are in-network, which makes it a viable option for people who want mental health covered under their existing telehealth benefit. The platform covers both therapy and psychiatry, with psychiatric evaluations being a particular strength. As a dedicated ongoing therapy platform, though, it's less compelling — the per-session model and general telehealth infrastructure mean you won't find the ongoing care features, async messaging, or between-session support that therapy-specific platforms offer.
best_for: Insured patients wanting mental health covered under telehealth benefits
People needing psychiatric evaluation or one-off urgent mental health appointments
Those who already use Doctor on Demand for general telehealth
look_elsewhere: If you want ongoing dedicated weekly therapy — use a therapy-specific platform
Budget self-pay users — per-session costs are high without insurance
Those wanting async messaging, coaching, or between-session support
pricing_detail: Doctor on Demand's self-pay rates are among the higher end of the telehealth market: -299 per session depending on provider type and appointment length. With insurance, your standard telehealth copay applies, which changes the math significantly. This platform makes the most financial sense for insured patients.
formats_detail: Video-only. Doctor on Demand's model is clinical appointment-based — you schedule a session, attend via video, and that's the encounter. There is no messaging subscription, no async support, and no between-session tools. Each appointment stands alone.
pull_quote: Doctor on Demand earns its place through insurance depth and reliability — not feature richness. For insured patients who need a known, covered option, that's often enough.

name: Done ADHD
tagline: Streamlined ADHD evaluation and medication — a fast online path to diagnosis
verdict_short: Done focuses narrowly on ADHD evaluation and medication management. Fast, relatively affordable pathway to an ADHD diagnosis and stimulant prescription online.
body: Done ADHD does one thing: ADHD evaluation and medication management. The platform is designed to reduce the friction between suspecting you have ADHD and actually getting assessed and treated. The evaluation costs  as a one-time fee; ongoing medication management is a monthly subscription. Done works with licensed psychiatric providers who specialize in ADHD. Insurance coverage is limited, but for people who've been on a six-month waitlist at a local psychiatrist, the accessibility gap Done closes is real. One important note: Done does prescribe stimulant medications, but the evaluation process is rigorous — this isn't a prescription mill.
best_for: Adults who suspect ADHD and want evaluation without a long waitlist
People seeking ongoing medication management after a diagnosis
Those who want a streamlined, ADHD-specific platform rather than a general psychiatrist
look_elsewhere: If you want therapy alongside medication — Done is medication-focused only
If you need comprehensive insurance coverage
If you prefer in-person evaluation over telehealth assessment
pricing_detail: Done charges  for the initial ADHD evaluation — a one-time fee that covers your assessment with a licensed provider. After diagnosis, ongoing medication management is /month. Insurance is not widely accepted, making this a primarily self-pay platform. Compared to a private psychiatrist (-500 for an evaluation), the math is favorable for self-pay patients.
formats_detail: The evaluation and medication management appointments are conducted via video. Between appointments, Done uses async communication for follow-up questions and refill management. The model is clinical rather than therapy-style — structured encounters with clear objectives.
pull_quote: The six-month waitlist for an ADHD evaluation at a local psychiatrist is a real barrier. Done's platform addresses the access problem directly — not perfectly, but meaningfully.
